segments.title: VLS count is a poor modern warship rating
segments.question: Could missile-armed surface combatants be rated by VLS count, with corvettes below 20 cells, frigates at 20-48, destroyers around 50-98, and cruisers above 100?
segments.answerShort: No. Dr. Clarke argues that destroyer and frigate labels depend more on role and capability than raw VLS count, especially because different VLS systems and missile loads are not equivalent.
segments.summary: A Bruships answer rejects classifying modern surface combatants by VLS count alone because roles, missile types, and VLS standards vary too much.
segments.body: This Q&A captures a concise modern-navy classification point from the restarted livestream. Dr. Clarke compares VLS counts with older rating systems, then stresses that missile load, VLS type, and ship role matter more than a simple cell-count bracket.

evidence.note: The audience question proposes a VLS-count rating scheme, and the answer rejects it because destroyers and frigates are role categories more than VLS-number categories.
The answer explains that VLS systems and missile loads are not equivalent, using different missile mixes and Type 45's Sylver fit as examples.
The follow-on discussion contrasts Sylver and Mark 41 choices, European standardization, Horizon-frigate commonality, and small differences that produce different capabilities.
